text: Di saha ka o daa pirig’ o pako’ chinchina sɔŋ ka zaŋ sariga pa m-mulis’ omaŋ’ chaŋ ti ʒini Ɛnaim dunoli din daa be Timna soli zuɣu la shee; dama o daa nyaya ka Shɛla bi doo, ka bɛ bi chɛ ka o kpuɣ’ o.

ref: When Judah came along, he did not recognize her because of the veil. He thought she was a prostitute

text: Ka Juda bɔh’ o, “Bɔ ka a lee bɔri ni n zaŋ ti a ka di nyɛ alikauli lɔbu shɛhira?” Ka paɣ’ maa garigi yɛl’ o, “Zaŋm’ a nintua din mali dalinli ŋɔ mini zoligo ŋɔ ni a jaaŋgbee din be a nuu ni ŋɔ ti ma.” Di saha ka o daa zaŋ li ti o, ka zaŋ o dɔni, ka o daa niŋ pua.

ref: After returning home, Tamar took off the veil and dressed in her widow's clothes again.
text: Di nyaaŋa ka paɣ’ maa daa yiɣisi kuli nti paai sariga maa sɔŋ, ka lan zaŋ o pako’ chinchina maa so.

ref: Judah asked his friend Hirah take a goat to the woman, so he could get back the ring and walking stick, but she wasn't there.
text: Ka Juda daa zaŋ bubil’ polli maa ti o zo ŋun daa nyɛ Adulam nir’ la ni o ti ti paɣ’ maa ka deeg’ o neen’ shɛŋa din daa nyɛ alikauli lɔbu shɛhira la na, amaa ka o daa bi nya paɣ’ maa.

ref: Hirah asked the people of Enaim, “Where is the prostitute who sat along the road outside your town?” “There's never been one here,” they answered.
text: Ka o daa bɔhi tiŋ’ maa ni dɔbba, “Ka jɛmbu duu pagɔr’ so ŋun yi ʒiini Inaim palli naŋgbanyee ŋɔ la lee be ya?” Ka bɛ yɛl’ o, “Pagɔr’ so na ʒi n-ka kpe na.”

ref: Hirah went back and told Judah, “I couldn't find the woman, and the people of Enaim said no prostitute had ever been there.”
text: Ka o daa lab’ ti yɛli Juda, “M bi nya paɣ’ maa; ka tiŋ’ maa nim’ yɛli ma ni pagɔr’ so na ʒi n-ka kpe na.”

ref: “If you couldn't find her, we'll just let her keep the things I gave her,” Judah answered. “And we'd better forget about the goat, or else we'll look like fools.”
text: Ka Juda daa yɛl’ o, “Chɛ ka o zaŋ nɛma maa leeg’ o dini, di yi pa lala, niriba ni la ti; nyama, n-chɛ ka a zaŋ bubil’ polli maa ni a ti ti o, ka a bi tooi bo n-nya o.”

ref: About three months later someone told Judah, “Your daughter-in-law Tamar has behaved like a prostitute, and now she's pregnant!” “Drag her out of town and burn her to death!” Judah shouted.
text: Zaŋ chaŋ chira ata saha ka so daa ti yɛli Juda, “Tama ŋun nyɛ a bia pakol’ la gɔrila pagɔrisigu, ka din pahi di zuɣu nyɛla, o niŋla pua o pagɔrisigu maa gɔbu ni.” Ka Juda daa yɛli, “Zaŋmiy’ o na ti niŋ buɣim nyo o.”

ref: As Tamar was being dragged off, she sent someone to tell her father-in-law, “The man who gave me this ring, this cord, and this walking stick is the one who got me pregnant.”
text: Bɛ ni daa zaŋ paɣ’ maa yina, ka o tim ti yɛl’ o yidan’ ba Juda, “Do’ so ŋun su nɛma ŋɔ n-ti ma pua maa. N suhir’ a, lihim’ li nya ŋuni n-lee su li. Nɛma maa n-nyɛ: nintua din mali dalinli ŋɔ mini zoligo ŋɔ ni jaaŋgbee ŋɔ.”

ref: “Those are mine!” Judah admitted. “She's a better person than I am, because I broke my promise to let her marry my son Shelah.” After this, Judah never slept with her again.
text: Di saha ka Juda daa baŋ nɛma maa ka yɛli, “Paɣ’ maa nyɛla wuntizɔra n-gari ma, n ni bi zaŋ o ti m bia Shɛla la zuɣu.” Ka o daa bi saɣi ka o mini paɣ’ maa lan dɔni yaha.

ref: Tamar later gave birth to twins. But before either of them was born, one of them stuck a hand out of her womb. The woman who was helping tied a red thread around the baby's hand and explained, “This one came out first.”
text: O ni daa wolisira, ka bi’ yino zaŋ o nuu tirina, ka paɣidɔɣisɔtɔɣinda daa zaŋ gali ʒee lo o nuu maa ka yɛli, “Ŋumbɔŋɔ ka bɛ daŋ dɔɣibu.”

ref: At once his hand went back in, and the other child was born first. The woman then said, “What an opening you've made for yourself!” So they named the baby Perez.
text: Amaa ka o daa fɔh’ o nuu maa kpɛhi, ka o mabia maa daŋ o yibuna; ka paɣidɔɣisɔtɔɣinda maa yɛli, “A niŋla kpaɣikpaɣi nya soli yina maa!” Dinzuɣu ka bɛ daa bol’ o yuli Pɛrɛz.

ref: When the brother with the red thread was born, they named him Zerah.
text: Dimbɔŋɔ nyaaŋa ka o mabia maa gba daa yina ka gali ʒee maa lo o nuu, ka bɛ daa bol’ o mi yuli Zɛra.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.
text: Ka o dan’ maa daa nya ka Yawɛ be Yisifu sani, ka chɛ ka o ni niŋdi shɛlikam zaa tɔŋda.

ref: Potiphar liked Joseph and made him his personal assistant, putting him in charge of his house and all of his property.
text: Dinzuɣu ka Pɔtifa suhu daa yu Yisifu pam, ka Yisifu daa tumd’ o tuma, ka o zaŋ o leeg’ o yiŋ’ tumtumdiba zuɣulana, ka zaŋ o binshɛɣukam zaa niŋ o nuu ni.

ref: Potiphar left everything up to Joseph, and with Joseph there, the only decision he had to make was what he wanted to eat. Joseph was well-built and handsome,
text: Dinzuɣu ka Pɔtifa daa zaŋ o ni mali shɛlikam zaa niŋ Yisifu nuu ni. Yisifu ni daa be o sani maa zuɣu, o baɣa daa lan ka shɛl’ ni n-naɣ’ pahila bindir’ shɛl’ o ni daa diri ni. Yisifu daa yi nachimbil’ viɛlli pam, ka o lihibu viɛla.

ref: and Potiphar's wife soon noticed him. She asked him to make love to her,
text: Ka di daa niŋ daba ayi ka Yisifu dan’ maa paɣ’ ti lihir’ o kɔre lihigu, ka yɛl’ o, “Chɛ ka m min’ a dɔni.”

ref: but he refused and said, “My master isn't worried about anything in his house, because he has placed me in charge of everything he owns.
text: Amaa ka Yisifu daa zaɣisi ka yɛl’ o dan’ paɣ’ maa, “Nyama, n ni be n dan’ sani ŋɔ, o baɣa lan ka shɛl’ ni yil’ ŋɔ. O zaŋ o ni mali shɛlikam niŋla n nuu ni.

ref: No one in my master's house is more important than I am. The only thing he hasn't given me is you, and that's because you are his wife. I won't sin against God by doing such a terrible thing as this.”
text: O bi galisi gari ma yili ŋɔ puuni, o mi bi mɔŋ ma shɛli naɣila nyini, dama a nyɛla o paɣa; ka wula ka n ni tooi tum ninvuɣ’ biɛtali tuun’ titali ŋɔ n-tum alahichi zaŋ chaŋ Naawuni sani?”

ref: She kept begging Joseph day after day, but he refused to do what she wanted or even to go near her.
text: Hal o ni daa kul yɛri Yisifu lala dabisili kam maa zaa yoli, Yisifu daa bi saɣi ni o min’ o dɔni.

ref: One day, Joseph went to Potiphar's house to do his work, and none of the other servants were there.
text: Amaa ka Yisifu daa ti kpe yili maa ni dahin’ shɛli ni o ti tum o tuma, ka ninvuɣ’ shɛb’ bɛn daa be yili maa ni kani.

ref: Potiphar's wife grabbed hold of his coat and said, “Make love to me!” Joseph ran out of the house, leaving his coat there in her hands.
text: Ka paɣ’ maa daa gbaai Yisifu binyɛrigu gbib’ ka yɛl’ o, “Chɛ ka m min’ a dɔni.” Amaa ka Yisifu daa fuɣ’ o binyɛrigu maa niŋ paɣ’ maa nuu ni, ka guui yi yili maa ni.

ref: she called in her servants and said, “Look! This Hebrew has come just to make fools of us. He tried to rape me, but I screamed for help.
text: ka o daa boli ninvuɣ’ shɛb’ bɛn daa be o yiŋ’ maa na nti yɛli ba, “Nyamiya, o ni zaŋ Hiburu nir’ maa na ka o ti lihi ʒiɛm ti shɛm, o kpɛmina ni o ti zaŋ ma dɔni, ka n kuhi pam.

ref: And when he heard me scream, he ran out of the house, leaving his coat with me.”
text: O ni wum ka n duhi n yee kuhi pam maa, ka o chɛ o binyɛrigu bahi ma ka guui yi yili ŋɔ.”

ref: Potiphar's wife kept Joseph's coat until her husband came home.
text: Di saha ka o daa zaŋ Yisifu binyɛrigu maa sɔŋ baɣil’ omaŋ’ hal ka Yisifu dan’ maa ti kuna.

ref: But when I screamed for help, he left his coat and ran out of the house.”
text: amaa n ni di kul duhi n yee kuhi pam saha shɛli, ka o chɛ o binyɛrigu ŋɔ bahi ma ka guui yi yili ŋɔ.”

ref: and threw Joseph in the same prison where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was in prison,
text: Ka Yisifu dan’ maa daa gbaag’ o n-zaŋ o ti niŋ sarika ni din nyɛ luɣ’ shɛli bɛ ni daa zaŋ naa sarikanim’ niŋ, ka o daa be sarika maa ni.

ref: the Lord helped him and was good to him. He even made the jailer like Joseph so much that
text: Amaa ka Naawuni daa be Yisifu sani ka niŋ o nirilim, ka chɛ ka dansarika gul’ maa suhu yu o.

ref: he put him in charge of the other prisoners and of everything that was done in the jail.
text: Ka dansarika gul’ maa daa zaŋ sarikanim’ bɛn zaa be sarika maa ni niŋ Yisifu nuu ni, ka Yisifu daa nyɛ ŋun niŋdi binshɛɣukam zaa nimaani.

ref: The jailer did not worry about anything, because the Lord was with Joseph and made him successful in all that he did.
text: Ka dansarika gul’ maa daa bi lan lihir’ o ni zaŋ binshɛlikam niŋ Yisifu nuu ni maa, dama Yawɛ daa be Yisifu sani, ka o daa yi niŋ shɛlikam, Yawɛ chɛrimi ka di tɔŋ.

ref: While Joseph was in prison, both the king's personal servant and his chief cook made the king angry. So he had them thrown into the same prison with Joseph.
text: ka o daa zaŋ ba ti kpɛhi duu o tɔbbihi bɛn daa gul’ o la sapashin’ kpɛm’ yiŋ’ luɣ’ shɛli bɛ ni daa zaŋ Yisifu ti niŋ maa.

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ka tɔbbihi sapashin’ kpɛm’ maa daa zaŋ ba niŋ Yisifu nuu ni ka o sɔŋdi ba. Ka bɛ daa be duu maa ni n-niŋ daba ayi.

ref: The next morning, when Joseph went to see the men, he could tell they were upset,
text: Ka Yisifu daa ti kpe bɛ sani asiba n-nya ka bɛ malila suhugarigu.

ref: “We each had a dream last night,” they answered, “and there is no one to tell us what they mean.” Joseph replied, “Doesn't God know the meaning of dreams? Now tell me what you dreamed.”
text: Ka bɛ yɛl’ o, “Ti sa zahimla zahima ka bi nya ŋun ni wuhi ti di gbinni.” Ka Yisifu bɔhi ba, “Pa Naawuni n-wuhiri zahindi gbinni? N suhiri ya, yɛlimiya ma yi zahima maa.”

ref: with three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its grapes became ripe.
text: ka tiwula ata be di zuɣu, ka di ni sa kul piligi puhiri vari ka dii daai pum n-wali wala ka di mooi.

ref: I held the king's cup and squeezed the grapes into it, then I gave the cup to the king.”
text: Ka Firawuna pipia daa be n nuu ni, ka m pɔhi wain tiwala maa kahim di kom niŋ Firawuna pipia maa ni n-zaŋ li ti ti ŋun’ Firawuna.”

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three branches stand for three days,
text: Di saha ka Yisifu daa yɛl’ o, “Di gbinni m-bɔŋɔ: Tiwula ata maa nyɛla daba ata.

ref: and in three days the king will pardon you. He will make you his personal servant again, and you will serve him his wine, just as you used to do.
text: Zaŋ chaŋ daba ata dali, Firawuna ni chɛ ka bɛ kana ti yih’ a nti lan zaŋ a labis’ a tuma ni; ka a ni lan zaŋdi Firawuna pipia maa ni binnyura maa ti tir’ o kaman a ni daa pun nyɛ o wain tɔɣira shɛm la.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Amaa di yi ti niŋ a viɛnyɛla, n suhir’ a, nyin’ teemi n yɛla, ka niŋ ma nirilim n-zaŋ n yɛtɔɣa tɔɣisi Firawuna; ka lala chɛ ka n yi sarika ŋɔ ni.

ref: I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and here in Egypt I haven't done anything to deserve being thrown in jail.
text: Dama yɛlimaŋli, bɛ daa zu ma la Hiburu tiŋgbɔŋ ni na; ka kpe gba, m bi tum taal’ shɛli ka di simdi ni bɛ zaŋ ma kpɛhi sarika.”

ref: When the chief cook saw that Joseph had given a good meaning to the dream, he told Joseph, “I also had a dream. In it I was carrying three breadbaskets stacked on top of my head.
text: Bɔrɔbɔro shɛriba kpɛm’ la ni daa wum di gbinni ka di viɛl’ maa, ka o daa yɛli Yisifu, “N gba sa zahimla zahindi. Bɔrɔbɔro piɛr’ ata n-sa tam n zuɣu ni,

ref: The top basket was full of all kinds of baked things for the king, but birds were eating them.”
text: ka bindira balibu kam din nyɛ bɛ ni daa she shɛli ti Firawuna be piɛ’ shɛli din tam zuɣusaa ni, amaa ka noombihi lee kana ti diri li n zuɣu ni piɛri maa ni.”

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three baskets are three days,
text: Ka Yisifu daa garigi yɛl’ o, “Di gbinni m-bɔŋɔ: Piɛr’ ata maa nyɛla daba ata.

ref: and in three days the king will cut off your head. He will hang your body on a pole, and birds will come and peck at it.
text: Zaŋ chaŋ daba ata dali, Firawuna ni chɛ ka bɛ kana ti yih’ a ti ŋma a zuɣu, ka zaŋ a yili tia ka noonsi di a nimdi.”

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,
text: amaa ka daa chɛ ka bɛ zaŋ o bɔrɔbɔro shɛriba kpɛm’ maa ti yil’ tia kaman Yisifu ni daa yɛli ba shɛm maa.

ref: but the king's personal servant completely forgot about Joseph.
text: Amaa di zaa yoli, wain booriba kpɛm’ la daa tam Yisifu yɛla ka bi teeg’ o yɛla.

ref: Two years later the king of Egypt dreamed he was standing beside the Nile River.
text: Yuma ayi nyaaŋa ka Firawuna daa ti zahim zahindi n-nya ka o ʒiɛla Nail Mɔɣili duli.

ref: Suddenly, seven fat, healthy cows came up from the river and started eating grass along the bank.
text: Ka naɣ’ kara ayopɔin bɛn niŋgbuna kul nyɛlisiri daa yi Nail Mɔɣili maa ni na ti ŋubiri mɔri.

ref: ate the fat, healthy cows. When this happened, the king woke up.
text: Ka naɣ’ kɔri bɛn daa gaŋsigaŋsi maa daa zaŋ naɣ’ kara ayopɔin bɛn niŋgbuna daa kul nyɛlisiri la ŋubi. Ka Firawuna daa yirigi neei.

ref: The king went back to sleep and had another dream. This time seven full heads of grain were growing on a single stalk.
text: Ka o daa lan gbihi n-zahim zahindi din pah’ ayi. Kawaan’ kara ayopɔin din viɛla n-daa nyaɣi kawaan’ kaɣ’ yini zuɣu.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Ka kawaan’ kɔr’ ayopɔin din nyɛ biisim polo pɔhim ni ŋme shɛli ka di zaɣi daa to n-yina pahi kawaan’ kara maa zuɣu.

ref: The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full heads. Again the king woke up, and it had only been a dream.
text: Ka kawaan’ kɔri maa daa zaŋ kawaan’ kara ayopɔin maa vali. Ka Firawuna daa yirigi neei n-nya ka di nyɛla zahindi.

ref: The next morning the king was upset. So he called in his magicians and wise men and told them what he had dreamed. None of them could tell him what the dreams meant.
text: Ka di daa muɣis’ o pam asibaasi, ka o tim ti boli Ijipti timanim’ mini baŋdiba zaa na, ka ŋun’ Firawuna daa yɛli ba o zahima maa, amaa ka so daa kani n-nyɛ ŋun ni tooi wuhi Firawuna o zahima maa gbinni.

ref: The king's personal servant said: Now I remember what I was supposed to do.
text: Di saha ka wain booriba kpɛm’ la daa yɛli Firawuna, “N teegi n galimi ni nyɛ shɛli zuŋɔ.

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Nyin’ Firawuna suhu ni daa ti yiɣisi bah’ a tumtumdiba zuɣu, ka a zaŋ m min’ a bɔrɔbɔro shɛriba kpɛm’ ti kpɛhi duu a tɔbbihi bɛn gul’ a sapashin’ kpɛm’ yiŋ’ la,

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: ti daa ti zahimla zahindi yuŋ dahin yini, ka ti zaa yino kam zahindi daa mali di gbinni.

ref: A young Hebrew, who was a servant of the captain of the guard, was there with us at the time. When we told him our dreams, he explained what each of them meant,
text: Ka Hiburu nachimbil’ so ŋun nyɛ a tɔbbihi bɛn gul’ a la sapashin’ kpɛm’ dabili daa be ti sani, ka ti yɛl’ o, ka o daa wuhi ti ti zaa yinoyino zahindi gbinni ni nyɛ shɛm.

ref: and everything happened just as he said it would. I got my job back, and the cook was put to death.
text: O ni daa kul wuhi ti di gbinni shɛm, di daa ti shiri niŋla pali; a daa labisi ma n tuma ni, ka chɛ ka bɛ zaŋ a bɔrɔbɔro shɛriba kpɛm’ la mi ti yili tia.”

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Di saha ka Firawuna daa tim ka bɛ chaŋ ti boli Yisifu n-zaŋ o sarika maa ni na yomyom; ka o daa pini o ninni ka taɣi binyɛra ye n-kpe Firawuna sani.

ref: The king said to him, “I had a dream, yet no one can explain what it means. I am told that you can interpret dreams.”
text: Ka Firawuna daa ti yɛli Yisifu, “N zahimla zahindi ka bi nya so ŋun ni wuhi ma di gbinni; ka n wum ka bɛ yɛli ma ni bɛ yi yɛl’ a zahindi ni nyɛ shɛm, a ni tooi wuhi di gbinni.”

ref: “Your Majesty,” Joseph answered, “I can't do it myself, but God can give a good meaning to your dreams.”
text: Ka Yisifu daa garigi yɛli Firawuna, “Pa mani n-wuhiri di gbinni maa, amaa Naawuni ni wuhi nyin’ Firawuna di gbinni ka di niŋ a nyaɣisim.”

ref: The king told Joseph: I dreamed I was standing on the bank of the Nile River.
text: Di saha ka Firawuna daa yɛli Yisifu, “N zahindi maa puuni, n daa ʒiɛla Nail Mɔɣili duli;

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: ka naɣ’ kara ayopɔin bɛn niŋgbuna kul nyɛlisiri daa yi Nail Mɔɣili maa ni na ti ŋubiri mɔri,

ref: Next, seven skinny, bony cows came up out of the river. I have never seen such terrible looking cows anywhere in Egypt.
text: ka naɣ’ kɔri bɛba ayopɔin shɛb’ bɛn kul gaŋsigaŋsi daa yina n-ka bɛ sanna, ka n nini daa na ʒi n-nya bɛ tatabo Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ ni.

ref: But you couldn't tell it, because these skinny cows were just as skinny as they were before. At once, I woke up.
text: amaa bɛ ni daa zaŋ ba ŋub’ maa, so daa naan ku tooi baŋ ni bɛ zaŋ ba ŋubi, dama bɛ daa na kul gaŋsigaŋsimi, ka be kaman bɛ ni daa pun be shɛm. Di nyaaŋa ka n daa yirigi neei.

ref: I also dreamed that I saw seven heads of grain growing on one stalk. The heads were full and ripe.
text: N zahindi maa puuni, n daa lan nya ka kawana ayopɔin nyaɣi tam kawaan’ kaɣ’ yini zuɣu n-kul ŋmɛlim baribara;

ref: Then seven other heads of grain came up. They were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: ka kawaan’ kɔr’ ayopɔin din nyɛ biisim ni kpe shɛli ka di kul zaɣizaɣimi to n-yina pahi di zuɣu,

ref: These heads of grain swallowed the full ones. I told my dreams to the magicians, but none of them could tell me the meaning of the dreams.
text: ka kawaan’ kɔr’ ayopɔin maa daa zaŋ kawaan’ kar’ ayopɔin maa vali. Ka n zaŋ li yɛli timanima, amaa ka bɛ ni so bi tooi wuhi ma di gbinni.”

ref: Joseph replied: Your Majesty, both of your dreams mean the same thing, and in them God has shown what he is going to do.
text: Di saha ka Yisifu daa yɛli Firawuna, “A zahima maa nyɛla zaɣ’ yini, Naawuni n-kahig’ o ni yɛn niŋ shɛli wuhi nyin’ Firawuna maa.

ref: The seven good cows stand for seven years, and so do the seven good heads of grain.
text: Naɣ’ kara bɛba ayopɔin la gbinni nyɛla yuma ayopɔin, ka kawaan’ kar’ ayopɔin la gba gbinni nyɛla yuma ayopɔin; zahindi maa kul nyɛla zaɣ’ yini.

ref: The seven skinny, ugly cows that came up later also stand for seven years, as do the seven bad heads of grain that were scorched by the desert wind. The dreams mean there will be seven years when there won't be enough grain.
text: Naɣ’ kɔri bɛba ayopɔin bɛn daa yina n-kul gaŋsigaŋsi la gbinni nyɛla yuma ayopɔin, ka kawaan’ kɔr’ ayopɔin din daa nyɛ biisim ni kpe shɛl’ la gba gbinni nyɛla kum lubu yuma ayopɔin.

ref: It is just as I said—God has shown what he intends to do.
text: Di nyɛla kaman n ni pun yɛli nyin’ Firawuna shɛm la, Naawuni n-kahig’ o ni yɛn niŋ shɛli wuh’ a.

ref: For seven years Egypt will have more than enough grain,
text: Yuma ayopɔin din kanna puuni, bindira ni niŋ pam Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ zaa ni.

ref: The famine will be so bad that no one will remember that once there had been plenty.
text: Ka so ti ku mi ni bindira daa niŋya pam kum shɛli din yɛn doli di nyaaŋa na maa zuɣu, dama kum maa ni ti mii pam.

ref: God has given you two dreams to let you know that he has definitely decided to do this and that he will do it soon.
text: Ka nyin’ Firawuna ni nya zahindi maa buyi la wuhiya ni Naawuni n-zal’ li, ka ni chɛ ka di ku yuui, ka di niŋ.

ref: Your Majesty, you should find someone who is wise and will know what to do, so that you can put him in charge of all Egypt.
text: “Dinzuɣu pumpɔŋɔ, piim’ ninvuɣ’ so ŋun mali dahin’ shɛli tɛha ni yɛm n-zaŋ Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ yɛla niŋ o nuu ni.

ref: Then appoint some other officials to collect one fifth of every crop harvested in Egypt during the seven years when there is plenty.
text: Di lan simdi ni a pii kpambal’ shɛb’ ka bɛ su tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ tuma fukumsi, ka bɛ ti laɣim bindira pirigibu bunu puuni yim zuɣu zali bindira ni ti yɛn niŋ pam yuma ayopɔin shɛŋa puuni la.

ref: Give them the power to collect the grain during those good years and to store it in your cities.
text: Ka a chɛ ka bɛ laɣim yuma ayopɔin shɛŋa din kanna ŋɔ ni bindira din yɛn niŋ pam maa zaa zali, ka di nyɛ a ni zaŋ bindir’ shɛli niŋ fukumsi fɔntinsi ni ka bɛ gbib’ li.

ref: It can be stored until it is needed during the seven years when there won't be enough grain in Egypt. This will keep the country from being destroyed because of the lack of food. Joseph Is Made Governor over Egypt
text: Ka lala bindira maa ni nyɛ a tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ ni bindira n-ʒiɛya gu yuma ayopɔin shɛŋa ni kum ni ti yɛn lu Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ ni maa, ka di chɛ ka kum ti ku ku tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ nima.” Bɛ zaŋ Yisifu leei Ijipti gomna

ref: The king and his officials liked this plan.
text: Ka Firawuna min’ o kpambaliba nini daa tiɣ’ o ni yɛli shɛm maa.

ref: So the king said to them, “Who could possibly handle this better than Joseph? After all, the Spirit of God is with him.”
text: Ka Firawuna daa bɔh’ o kpambaliba maa, “Ti ni tooi nya ninvuɣ’ so ka o be kaman Naawuni Shia ni be do’ so ni ŋɔ?”

ref: The king told Joseph, “God is the one who has shown you these things. No one else is as wise as you are or knows as much as you do.
text: Di saha ka Firawuna daa yɛli Yisifu, “Naawuni ni wuh’ a dimbɔŋɔ zaa maa zuɣu, so kani m-mali tɛhi’ wɔɣila ni yɛm kaman nyini.

ref: I'm putting you in charge of my palace, and everybody will have to obey you. No one will be over you except me.
text: Nyini n-yɛn su n yili ŋɔ fukumsi, ka a yi kul yɛli shɛlikam, n niriba zaa ni niŋ li; kulla n nam ʒiishee ŋɔ ka n yɛn zaŋ gar’ a.”

ref: Then the king took off his royal ring and put it on Joseph's finger. He gave him fine clothes to wear and placed a gold chain around his neck.
text: Ka ŋun’ Firawuna daa pirig’ o nuu ni nintu’ shɛli o ni mali dalindi binyara n-zaŋ li piri Yisifu, ka zaŋ bimpiɛl’ viɛlli kparibu yɛl’ o, ka zaŋ salima dolibi yɛl’ o;

ref: He also let him ride in the chariot next to his own, and people shouted, “Make way for Joseph!” So Joseph was governor of Egypt.
text: ka chɛ ka o kpe n-ʒin’ o chɛchɛbuŋ’ din pah’ ayi puuni, ka chɛ ka bɛn be o tooni kuhiri yɛra, “Damdimiy’ o tiŋa!” Lala ka o daa niŋ zaŋ Yisifu leei Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ maa zaa zuɣulana.

ref: The king told Joseph, “Although I'm king, no one in Egypt is to do anything without your permission.”
text: Ka din pahi nyɛla, Firawuna daa lan yɛli Yisifu, “Mani n-nyɛ Firawuna, so ku tooi kpuɣ’ o nuu zuɣusaa bee n-vuug’ o napɔŋ Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ zaa ni, ka pa ni nyini m-bah’ o noli.”

ref: He gave Joseph the Egyptian name Zaphenath Paneah. And he let him marry Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera, a priest in the city of Heliopolis. Joseph traveled all over Egypt.
text: Ka Firawuna daa boli Yisifu yuli Zafɛnatipania, ka zaŋ Pɔtifɛra ŋun daa nyɛ Hɛliopolis tindan’ la bipuɣiŋga Asɛnat n-ti Yisifu ni o niŋ o paɣa. Ka Yisifu daa yiri chani gindi Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ maa.

ref: Joseph collected and stored up the extra grain in the cities of Egypt near the fields where it was harvested.
text: Ka Yisifu daa laɣim yuma ayopɔin maa bindira zaa din daa niŋ pam Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ ni maa n-sɔŋ fɔntinsi ni, ka laɣim fɔntiŋ’ kam ni puri ni bindira n-sɔŋ lala tinsi maa ni.
